stepA.reason: Today is Sunday UTC; weekly skill availability recheck runs on Monday only.

sources.title: Pentagon uses GenAI.mil to create 100K agents
sources.summary: Defense officials said GenAI.mil has more than 1.2 million users and was used to create 100,000 agents, signaling a shift from experimentation to scaled agent deployment inside DoD workflows.

sources.title: AI.mil homepage continues to foreground Agent Network / GenAI.mil / Enterprise Agents / WDP
sources.summary: Official AI.mil positioning still centers Agent Network, GenAI.mil, Enterprise Agents and War Data Platform, indicating the Pentagon’s AI stack remains organized around data layer + agent deployment + warfighting enablement.

sources.title: State Department advances Enterprise Data and AI Strategy and M-25-21 compliance plan
sources.summary: State says its three-year Enterprise Data and AI Strategy meets OMB M-25-21 requirements and pairs AI adoption with governance boards, use-case inventories, risk controls and termination of non-compliant AI use cases.

sources.title: AI.gov continues to present a top-level White House AI strategy posture
sources.summary: AI.gov remains active as a public top-layer policy signal, reinforcing that federal AI activity is being framed as national strategic leadership rather than isolated agency experimentation.

sources.title: Trump picked a fight with Anthropic. Now the administration is backing off.
sources.summary: Mainstream reporting indicates the administration’s pressure campaign around military use of Anthropic models may be softening, but the dispute itself confirms model-access terms can become a live defense procurement variable.

sources.title: ITAR & AI-Enabled Defense Technologies: Autonomous Systems, Targeting Algorithms, And The New Export-Control Frontier
sources.summary: Legal and policy coverage continues to treat AI-enabled defense systems as a new export-control frontier, reinforcing that model outputs, targeting systems and autonomous capabilities face growing trade-control scrutiny.

sources.title: Military AI Policy by Contract: The Limits of Procurement as Governance
sources.summary: Policy discussion continues to frame procurement clauses and contract language as the practical mechanism for AI restrictions, governance and vendor obligations inside military and federal projects.

supply_chain_mapping.policy_action: OMB M-25-21 compliance rollout inside State Department
supply_chain_mapping.delivery_impact: Higher governance and audit burden; projects need clearer use-case inventory, risk controls and documented compliance before scale-up.
supply_chain_mapping.reason: Not a ban, but raises entry and acceptance thresholds for federal AI delivery.

supply_chain_mapping.policy_action: Anthropic military-use dispute under Hegseth-era pressure
vendors_affected: prime contractors depending on Claude access
supply_chain_mapping.delivery_impact: Model availability, usage rights and contract clauses can change mid-cycle, forcing fallback models or re-approval paths.
supply_chain_mapping.reason: This hits model access directly, which can stall procurement and delivery if a chosen model becomes contractually constrained.

supply_chain_mapping.policy_action: AI-enabled defense tech increasingly framed under export-control frontier / ITAR lens
supply_chain_mapping.delivery_impact: Overseas deployment windows narrow; export reviews, substitution plans and sovereign/local alternatives become more important.
supply_chain_mapping.reason: Export controls are hard constraints that directly affect hardware supply, model access and international delivery paths.

fallbacks.browser: not used this round; web_fetch and RSS provided enough coverage for completion
